stem: Your team is adopting a GitHub MCP server for a developer-productivity agent. Every developer who clones the repository should get the server configuration automatically, but the GitHub token is per-developer and must never be committed to version control. Two developers also run personal, experimental MCP servers that the rest of the team should not inherit. How should you configure this?
correct_choice: Define the GitHub server in a project-level .mcp.json checked into the repository, referencing the token as ${GITHUB_TOKEN} via environment variable expansion, and have developers register their experimental servers at user level in ~/.claude.json.
correct_rationale: Project-scoped .mcp.json is version-controlled and shared with everyone who clones the repo, while environment variable expansion like ${GITHUB_TOKEN} resolves credentials from each developer's environment at connection time so no secret is ever committed. User-level ~/.claude.json is exactly the scope for personal or experimental servers that should not leak to teammates.
near_miss_choice: Have each developer add the GitHub server to their user-level ~/.claude.json with their token inlined, since user scope keeps credentials out of the repository.
near_miss_rationale: User scope does keep tokens out of version control, but it abandons the requirement that the shared server arrive automatically with a clone: every developer must hand-configure it, configurations drift, and new teammates silently lack the integration. Project-scoped .mcp.json with env expansion achieves both goals at once.
distractor1_choice: Commit the .mcp.json with the token embedded directly, relying on the repository being private to protect the credential.
distractor1_rationale: Committing a live credential to version control is a leak regardless of repository visibility: it persists in git history, is exposed to every collaborator and CI system, and cannot be rotated per developer. Environment variable expansion exists precisely so shared server config and private credentials can be separated.
distractor2_choice: Document the server command and token setup in CLAUDE.md so the agent reads the instructions and connects to the server itself.
distractor2_rationale: CLAUDE.md provides instructions and project context to the model; it is not a server registration mechanism. MCP servers are discovered from configuration files like .mcp.json or ~/.claude.json at connection time, not launched by the model reading prose documentation.
anti_pattern: Managing shared MCP credentials by either committing secrets to version control or falling back to unshared per-user configuration, instead of separating team-shared server definitions (project .mcp.json) from per-developer secrets (environment variable expansion).

stem: Your developer-productivity agent integrates with your issue tracker through a custom MCP server that exposes search_issues and get_issue tools. Session transcripts show the agent burning its first several turns on exploratory search_issues calls just to discover what projects, labels, and open epics exist before it can address the user's actual request. You want the agent to know what content is available up front without those discovery calls. What is the best change to the MCP server?
correct_choice: Expose the catalog — project list, label taxonomy, and open epic summaries — as MCP resources that can be attached or read directly, keeping tools for targeted actions like fetching or searching specific issues.
correct_rationale: MCP resources are the mechanism for exposing content catalogs (issue summaries, documentation hierarchies, database schemas) so the agent has visibility into available data without spending tool calls on exploration. Tools remain the right surface for parameterized actions; resources answer the standing question of what exists.
near_miss_choice: Add a list_everything tool that returns all projects, labels, epics, and issue summaries in one response, and instruct the agent to call it at the start of every session.
near_miss_rationale: This still spends an exploratory tool call every session and dumps the entire catalog into context whether or not it is relevant. It converts many discovery calls into one large one rather than eliminating discovery, which is exactly what exposing the catalog as MCP resources accomplishes.
distractor1_choice: Embed the current list of projects, labels, and epics into the search_issues tool description so the agent sees it whenever it inspects its tools.
distractor1_rationale: Tool descriptions are static configuration meant to explain purpose, inputs, and boundaries. Stuffing live catalog data into them bloats every request, goes stale the moment the tracker changes, and abuses the description field as a data channel.
distractor2_choice: Add a system prompt instruction telling the agent to remember project and label names from previous sessions instead of re-discovering them.
distractor2_rationale: Sessions do not share conversation state, so the agent has nothing to remember from prior sessions; the instruction invites the model to fabricate plausible-sounding project names. Availability of content must come from the server surface, not from imagined memory.

stem: Your Agent SDK productivity agent (with Read, Write, Bash, Grep, and Glob) is asked where the feature flag ENABLE_ASYNC_BILLING is handled in a large, unfamiliar monorepo, and how the value flows from configuration to the billing module. Which tool strategy should the agent use?
correct_choice: Grep for ENABLE_ASYNC_BILLING across the repo to find the entry points, then Read those specific files and follow their imports with further targeted Grep and Read calls to trace the flow.
correct_rationale: Grep is the content-search tool, so it locates every file that references the flag regardless of file name. Building understanding incrementally — search for entry points, then Read only the implicated files and follow imports — traces the data flow without flooding context with irrelevant code.
near_miss_choice: Glob for file names containing billing or flags (e.g., **/*billing*, **/*flag*), then Read each match to look for the constant.
near_miss_rationale: Glob matches file path patterns, not file contents. The flag may be referenced in files whose names mention neither billing nor flags (config loaders, DI wiring, tests), so name-based discovery both misses handlers and pulls in files that never touch the constant. Content search is Grep's job.
distractor1_choice: Read every file under src/ upfront so the agent has complete context before answering, then trace the flag from memory.
distractor1_rationale: Reading a large repository wholesale exhausts the context window on code that is irrelevant to the question and degrades reasoning quality. The effective pattern is incremental: search first, read only what the search implicates.
distractor2_choice: Ask the user to paste the relevant configuration and billing files into the conversation, since they know the codebase best.
distractor2_rationale: The premise is an unfamiliar codebase and an agent equipped with search tools; deferring discovery to the user defeats the purpose of codebase-exploration tooling and fails when the user does not know which files are relevant — which is the reason they asked.

stem: Your agent must change the timeout in one specific handler inside a generated service file. The line timeout_ms = 5000 appears six times, and because the handlers are code-generated, the surrounding lines are byte-identical in every block. The agent's Edit call fails because the target text does not match uniquely. What should the agent do?
correct_choice: Read the full file, construct the corrected content with only the intended handler's value changed, and Write the file back.
correct_rationale: When Edit cannot anchor on a unique text match, Read followed by Write is the reliable fallback: the agent sees the whole file, can identify the intended block positionally, and writes back a complete, verified result. This is the documented recovery path for non-unique matches.
near_miss_choice: Retry Edit with a larger snippet that includes several surrounding lines, giving the match more context to latch onto.
near_miss_rationale: Expanding the anchor is the right first instinct when nearby lines differ, but the stem states the surrounding lines are byte-identical across all six generated blocks, so a larger snippet is still non-unique and Edit fails the same way. That is precisely the situation where the Read + Write fallback applies.
distractor1_choice: Use Bash to run sed -i 's/timeout_ms = 5000/timeout_ms = 30000/' on the file to make the change in one step.
distractor1_rationale: An unanchored global substitution edits all six occurrences, changing the timeout of five handlers that must keep their current value. It trades a failed edit for a silent incorrect one.
distractor2_choice: Delete the file and Write a fresh copy from the agent's memory of its contents, incorporating the new timeout.
distractor2_rationale: Reconstructing a file from the model's recollection rather than a fresh Read invites omissions and hallucinated details, and deleting first removes the safety net if the rewrite is wrong. The fallback is Read-then-Write, never write-from-memory.
